name: Conall Cernach
description: Conall Cernach, meaning 'Conall the Victorious', was a celebrated warrior of the Ulster Cycle in Irish mythology. He was a foster-brother of Cú Chulainn and a loyal member of the Red Branch knights. Renowned for his unwavering bravery, ferocity in battle, and unyielding loyalty, Conall was considered one of the greatest heroes of Ulster. He was known for his oath to never let a day pass without slaying a Connachtman, nor sleep a night without a Connachtman's head beneath his knee. Conall played a crucial role in many of the great battles of the Ulster Cycle, often acting as Cú Chulainn's second in command. His most famous feat was avenging Cú Chulainn's death by pursuing and killing Lugaid mac Con Roi and Erc, the men responsible. This act solidified his reputation as a fierce protector of his friends and a relentless enemy to his foes. He is a symbol of unwavering loyalty, vengeance, and martial prowess in Irish lore, embodying the values of the warrior class in ancient Ireland. He met his end through treachery, showcasing the unpredictable nature of fate even for the mightiest of heroes.
subtitle: Victorious Warrior of the Ulster Cycle
achievements: Participating in the Táin Bó Cúailnge
Upholding the honor of the Red Branch

keyMyths.title: The Death of Cú Chulainn and Conall's Vengeance
keyMyths.description: When Cú Chulainn was killed, Conall Cernach relentlessly pursued his killers, Lugaid mac Con Roi and Erc, ultimately slaying them both to avenge his foster-brother's death. This act demonstrated his unwavering loyalty and ferocity.

keyMyths.title: Conall's Oath
keyMyths.description: Conall Cernach swore an oath to never let a day pass without killing a Connachtman and to never sleep without a Connachtman's head beneath his knee. This oath reflects his dedication to defending Ulster.

keyMyths.title: Conall in the Táin Bó Cúailnge
keyMyths.description: During the Táin Bó Cúailnge (Cattle Raid of Cooley), Conall Cernach fought alongside Cú Chulainn and the other warriors of Ulster, playing a vital role in defending their territory against Queen Medb's army.

sources.author: Thomas Kinsella
sources.description: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge.
sources.text: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge.

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz
